response:
the problem asks us to find the denominator of the fraction formed by dividing one hundred factorial by ten to the power of fifty.
***
to solve this we must first determine how many factors of ten are present in the prime factorization of one hundred factorial.
***
since ten is the product of two and five, the number of tens is limited by the count of prime factor five in the factorial.
***
we can store the count of fives by dividing one hundred by five, then by twenty five, and summing the results to get twenty four.
***
this means one hundred factorial contains exactly twenty four factors of ten, which is far fewer than the fifty required in the divisor.
***
when we divide by ten to the power of fifty we are left with ten to the power of twenty six in the denominator after simplification.
***
we must store this remaining power of ten because the numerator will no longer contain any factors of two or five to cancel it out.
***
the irreducible fraction will therefore have a denominator that is the product of two to the power of twenty six and five to the power of twenty six.
***
this calculation allows us to store the final value as ten to the power of twenty six, which is the answer to the original question.
***
the final denominator is ten raised to the twenty sixth power, and we have now stored the complete solution for this mathematical puzzle.
